cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure this out. The question is about why a novelist can expect the publisher to print their manuscript exactly as written. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I'll need to refer back to the passage provided. The passage starts by talking about the novelist's medium being the written word and how they have control over their text. It mentions that the novelist keeps absolute control until it's published. It also says that even if the writer doesn't want to revise based on the editor's advice, people wouldn't be surprised. Established novelists might deliver their manuscript expecting it to be printed as is. Looking at the options: A) Because the novelist keeps absolute control over his text. The passage does say that the novelist keeps absolute control until it's published. So maybe this is the reason. C) The novel is limited to a single channel of information. The passage says that the novel is limited to writing, which allows it to go anywhere without practical concerns.
